turns.user_message: I chew when I'm waiting for passengers at red lights, or during long trips when I'm driving on the highway, and sometimes even after I eat. My hands get bored and I need something to keep me occupied.
step2_rag_chunks: Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

step2_rag_chunks: When a patient has tried to quit multiple times and keeps relapsing, or reports strong physical cravings that are hard to manage, nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum or patches is worth discussing as an additional support tool alongside behavioral strategies.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
